title: svPITE: A Python package for the state-vector-based probabilistic imaginary-time evolution algorithm
abstract: We present a Python package for ground-state preparation based on the probabilistic imaginary-time evolution algorithm, with particular focus on its state-vector-based implementation. A standard shot-based simulation is also supported, and results can be benchmarked against exact diagonalisation via a dedicated wrapper. The package enables efficient tuning of initial parameters, facilitating systematic exploration and optimisation of the method's performance. Starting from the prepared ground state, the strong interoperability with other packages further enables real-time evolution and the computation of spectral functions, such as the spin-spin dynamical structure factor.
summary: The paper introduces svPITE, a Python package designed to implement the probabilistic imaginary-time evolution algorithm for quantum many-body systems. It offers both state-vector and shot-based simulation modes, allowing researchers to optimize algorithm parameters and benchmark quantum algorithms against exact classical results. The package is particularly useful for preparing ground states and computing dynamical spectral functions in spin models.
main_problem: The need for efficient ground-state preparation and thermal state simulations in quantum many-body physics, specifically addressing the challenge of implementing non-unitary imaginary-time evolution on quantum devices.
main_result: The development of the svPITE Python package, which provides an efficient state-vector-based implementation of the probabilistic imaginary-time evolution algorithm that converges to exact results in the infinite-shot limit.
method: A Python-based implementation of the Probabilistic Imaginary-Time Evolution (PITE) algorithm, featuring both state-vector-based (infinite-shot limit) and shot-based (stochastic) simulation modes, integrated with Qiskit and QuSpin.
model_or_system: Quantum many-body spin systems, including 1D and 2D Heisenberg models (XXZ) and Transverse Field Ising Models (TFIM) with periodic boundary conditions.
key_observables: Ground-state energy per site, success probability, infidelity, and spectral functions such as the spin-spin dynamical structure factor.
important_parameters: The PITE tuning parameter gamma, imaginary-time step size delta-tau, Trotter order, and the number of shots for shot-based simulations.
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian class is currently limited to single-site and two-site Pauli couplings (e.g., XX, YY, ZZ) and does not yet support more complex mixed terms like XY.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the PITE quantum circuit, comparisons of energy convergence and success probability between shot-based and state-vector methods, parameter sweeps of gamma, and the computation of the dynamic structure factor.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of non-unitary evolution, describes the svPITE software architecture and Hamiltonian representation, presents validation against Exact Diagonalization, demonstrates performance benchmarks for 1D and 2D models, and showcases applications in calculating dynamical correlation functions.
why_interesting: It provides a highly optimized numerical tool for simulating many-body dynamics and ground-state properties, bridging the gap between classical state-vector benchmarks and realistic shot-based quantum device simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: (a) Success probability P0 as a function of imaginary time steps nsteps for the state-vector-based (blue) and shot-based (red) implementations of the PITE algo- rithm, obtained using the example code provided in this section. (b) Corresponding energy per site E0/L as a function of nsteps for the state-vector-based simulation, as well as the final energy estimate from the shot-based PITE simulation including its standard deviation. The exact ground-state energy per site obtained by ED is shown as a black dashed line.

Figure 2 compares the results obtained with the example code using the state-vector-based and shot-based PITE simulations, along with the ED reference. The computation was per- formed in approximately 2 minutes on a MacBook Pro with an Apple M1 Max processor. The full code, including the plotting routines, is provided in App. C.

Figure 3: State-vector PITE simulations for different values of γ for the spin-1/2 Heisenberg chain with L = 16, using a singlet initial state and ∆τ = 0.2 with a first-order Trotter decomposition. (a) Single-step postselection success probability P0 and (b) approximate ground-state energy per site E0/L as functions of the num- ber of imaginary-time steps nsteps (i.e., PITE steps) for each γ. The exact ground-state energy EED/L is shown as a dashed horizontal line. (c) Cumulative success probabil- ity Q

Figure 4: (a) Success probability P0 and (b) approximate ground-state energy per site E0/L as functions of the number of steps, obtained using the shot-based PITE implementation with γ = 0.53 chosen from Fig. 3. The simulations are performed for the spin-1/2 Heisenberg chain with L = 16, using a singlet initial state and ∆τ = 0.2. Results obtained with 5,000 shots are shown in red and with 50,000 shots in blue. The solid black line indicates the state-vector PITE reference, while the dashed horizontal line denotes the ED energy. The plot was produced using the scripts in examples/heisenberg_shot_vs_sv/.

Figure 5: (a) Energy per site E0/N and (b) success probability P0 as functions of imaginary-time steps, nstep, obtained with the state-vector-based PITE algorithm in the 2D Heisenberg model on a 4×4 square lattice (i.e., N = 16 sites) with PBC. These plots were generated using the script examples/heisenberg_2D_ed_sv.py.
